description: Archived discoveries capture the validated learning that informed current bike-mobility delivery choices and current-quarter goals.

riskFramework.question: Will customers buy the product and/or choose to use it?
Will the solution meet the needs of the business?
Can customers figure out how to use it?
Can we build it within given resources and constraints?

items.title: Employer launch model: validated discovery
items.name: Employer launch model: validated discovery
items.summary: Discovery confirmed that employer adoption is constrained less by commercial interest than by payroll, policy, and launch friction, which makes rollout standardization a high-value product investment.
items.opportunity: Clarify whether standardizing employer policy setup, payroll integration, and billing control would materially reduce time to launch and improve employer confidence.

linkedInitiatives.description: Employer launch acceleration initiative focused on improving employer policy & program management, eligibility, payroll & HRIS sync, and settlement, invoicing & payroll reconciliation to make multi-country employer rollouts faster and less manual across Employer Admin Portal, Employer Integration API, and Employer KPI Cockpit.

assignments.how: Led the discovery framing, employer interviews, and synthesis that shaped the launch acceleration initiative.
Validated the integration constraints and the level of standardization possible across payroll and HRIS systems.
Validated billing, settlement, and payroll reconciliation pain points that slowed launches.
Helped define which exception-heavy launch tasks could be turned into reusable workflow states.

riskFocus.question: Will customers buy the product and/or choose to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Employers will adopt the platform faster if rollout no longer feels like a bespoke implementation project.
evidenceToCollect: Whether HR and mobility leaders describe launch friction as a primary barrier to expansion.
Whether faster launch readiness changes employer willingness to expand into additional countries.

riskFocus.question: Will the solution meet the needs of the business?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Lower launch effort and better rollout control will improve employer retention enough to justify investment in a stronger setup model.
evidenceToCollect: What retention and expansion signals are tied to launch quality.
How much manual rollout effort can realistically be removed.

riskFocus.question: Can customers figure out how to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Employer admins can configure and validate a launch flow with less support if the system is clearer and more guided.
evidenceToCollect: Where employer admins get stuck during policy setup and data validation.
What checklist and status information they expect before go-live.

riskFocus.question: Can we build it within given resources and constraints?
riskFocus.hypothesis: The teams can standardize the highest-friction launch steps without overhauling every country process at once.
evidenceToCollect: Which steps can be templated now and which still require local exceptions.
Whether integration sequencing can be broken into reusable rollout stages.

plannedActivities: Interview employer admins from recently launched and delayed programs.
Map the current end-to-end launch workflow across policy, payroll, and billing.
Define the smallest standard launch package for the next quarter.
items.decisionToMake: Decide whether to fund a launch acceleration initiative centered on standardization rather than ad hoc implementation support.

outcome.summary: Discovery showed enough employer pain and enough standardization headroom to proceed with a focused launch-acceleration initiative.

items.title: Rider conversion and trust: validated discovery
items.name: Rider conversion and trust: validated discovery
items.summary: Discovery confirmed that rider drop-off is concentrated around affordability ambiguity, opaque order progress, and low confidence in service recovery after delivery.
items.opportunity: Clarify whether improving quote transparency, order visibility, and rider self-service would materially improve rider conversion and early confidence.

linkedInitiatives.description: Rider conversion and trust initiative focused on improving bike catalog & recommendation, order orchestration & checkout, rider identity, wallet & app experience, and communications & notifications across iOS App, Android App, Rider Web Experience, and Lifecycle Notifications.

assignments.how: Led rider interviews, drop-off analysis, and the recommendation on where conversion and trust work should focus first.
Validated mobile-specific friction in quote exploration, status visibility, and rider support entry points.
Validated how notifications and lifecycle visibility affect rider trust after order confirmation.
Provided dealer and handover constraints that shape rider expectations after checkout.

riskFocus.question: Will customers buy the product and/or choose to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Employees who already like the bike benefit still need clearer financial and operational certainty before they commit.
evidenceToCollect: Where riders abandon the process during quote and checkout.
Whether better status transparency changes willingness to complete the order.

riskFocus.question: Will the solution meet the needs of the business?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Better conversion and lower trust-related support burden create enough value to justify dedicated rider-experience investment.
evidenceToCollect: What portion of support demand is caused by poor transparency rather than true operational failure.
How rider drop-off affects the active-rider base and early lifecycle value.

riskFocus.question: Can customers figure out how to use it?
riskFocus.hypothesis: Riders can complete the core quote-to-order journey with much less anxiety if cost and next steps are explicit.
evidenceToCollect: Where riders misread cost, timing, or service expectations.
Which order states need plain-language explanations.

riskFocus.question: Can we build it within given resources and constraints?
riskFocus.hypothesis: The teams can improve trust materially without rebuilding the full commerce stack.
evidenceToCollect: Which improvements can be delivered via order-state visibility, message orchestration, and self-service first.
Where partner dependencies will still limit end-to-end control.

plannedActivities: Interview recent riders who abandoned after creating a quote.
Review order status touchpoints and support tickets from the first month after order confirmation.
Test a clearer rider timeline for affordability, order progress, and handover readiness.
items.decisionToMake: Decide whether to proceed with a rider trust initiative focused on visibility and lifecycle confidence rather than purely acquisition messaging.

outcome.summary: Discovery showed that conversion and post-order confidence problems are concrete enough to justify a dedicated rider trust initiative.
